openehr.rps_dual.name: Reversed Path Search - Dual Collection
openehr.rps_dual.summary: A balanced approach to clinical data storage that keeps your original records intact while enabling fast searches across your entire patient population.
openehr.rps_dual.description: This strategy preserves the complete clinical narrative in each patient record while maintaining a streamlined search layer for population health queries, research, and analytics.

config_schema.title: openEHR RPS-Dual Strategy Configuration
config_schema.description: Data model and storage configuration for the Reversed Path Search dual-collection strategy. Visible in portal.

search.description: Slim search store for cross-patient queries

codes.description: Dictionary collection for path code mappings

mode.description: fresh: start from scratch; extend: reuse and continue sequence

fields.description: Field naming conventions for stored documents

pi.description: Path instance chain (list indexes aligned with path segments)

mappings.description: Field mappings: file://path, collection://name, or inline object

store_original.description: Keep original at-code alongside numeric code

benefits: Complete clinical records are preserved exactly as documented, supporting full audit trails, legal requirements, and the clinical fidelity that healthcare demands.
Population searches run fast—cohort identification, quality metrics, and research queries complete in seconds even across large patient populations.
You get a single system for both patient care and analytics, eliminating the ETL pipelines and data warehouses that traditionally create synchronization headaches and data drift.
Standard AQL queries work seamlessly throughout, automatically routed to whichever storage layer serves them best.

cards.title: Querying
cards.body: Dual pipelines: $match for patient scope, $search for population with slim docs.

cards.title: Ingest
cards.body: Stores canonical compositions and optional slim search documents.

ui.story:
Clinical teams need to access individual patient histories quickly and completely, while research and population health teams need to search across thousands of patients efficiently. This strategy solves both needs by storing the full clinical record for patient care, alongside a compact search layer optimized for population queries.

When a clinician opens a patient chart, they get the complete record exactly as it was documented—every observation, every medication, every note preserved with full fidelity. When a researcher searches for patients with specific conditions, the system uses an optimized search layer to return results in seconds rather than minutes, without ever compromising the integrity of the source data.

The result is a single source of truth that serves both operational and analytical workloads. Clinical teams trust it for patient care because nothing is lost or transformed. Research teams trust it for population health because queries are fast and comprehensive. IT teams appreciate it because there's no ETL pipeline to maintain, no data warehouse to synchronize, and no risk of operational and analytical systems drifting apart.
ui.how_it_works:
Your data lives in two synchronized collections that work together seamlessly. The primary collection stores complete clinical documents—each composition preserved as a single document with every field, every nested structure, exactly as the source system created it. This collection is optimized for patient-scoped queries: when you need everything about one patient, the system retrieves it instantly using efficient B-tree indexes.

The search collection maintains a streamlined projection of the same data, purpose-built for population queries. It contains just enough information to find patients matching complex criteria—diagnoses, medications, lab values, dates—without the overhead of full document storage. Atlas Search indexes this collection, enabling sub-second queries across millions of records.

The system automatically routes each query to the right collection. Ask for a specific patient's records, and it goes to the primary collection. Search for all patients with a particular condition, and it uses the search collection. You write standard AQL queries; the routing happens transparently.
ui.ideal_for: This strategy works best when your team needs both fast patient lookups and population-wide searches from the same data. It's particularly valuable when you want to run analytics directly on your operational data without maintaining separate systems, and when regulatory requirements demand that clinical records remain complete and unmodified. Healthcare organizations building clinical data repositories, research institutions conducting population health studies, and quality improvement teams tracking outcomes across patient cohorts all find this approach fits their needs.
ui.consider_alternatives: If you only need patient-level queries without population searches, the simpler single-collection strategy may serve you better with less operational overhead. If Atlas Search is not available in your MongoDB deployment, you'll need to evaluate whether the population query capabilities justify the infrastructure requirements.

fhir.contextobjects.vitals_window.name: FHIR ContextObjects - Vitals Window
fhir.contextobjects.vitals_window.summary: Turn high-frequency vitals streams (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2) into time-windowed ContextObjects where each node carries a standard FHIR Observation payload.
fhir.contextobjects.vitals_window.description: This strategy models wearable/monitor numeric signals as one ContextObject per patient + device + time window. Inside the ContextObject, each measurement becomes a node whose data is a FHIR Observation. The result is a consistent unit of storage and retrieval that remains FHIR-aligned and is easy to query and enrich in MongoDB.

config_schema.title: FHIR ContextObjects Vitals Window Strategy Configuration
config_schema.description: Configures ingestion and ContextObject materialization for vitals as FHIR Observation nodes.

timezone.description: Timezone used to compute buckets (keep UTC for deterministic behavior).

display.default: Oxygen saturation in Arterial blood by Pulse oximetry

spo2.display: Oxygen saturation in Arterial blood by Pulse oximetry

benefits: FHIR-aligned payloads: each measurement is a FHIR Observation
Predictable retrieval: one document gives you a full time window
Extensible: add derived metrics, quality flags, or embeddings later without changing the contract
Practical: simple model that works well for operational use cases

cards.title: Unit of record
cards.body: One ContextObject per patient + device + time window.

cards.title: Node payload
cards.body: Each node stores a FHIR Observation JSON payload.

cards.title: Transform
cards.body: CSV rows → window bucketing → Observation nodes → ContextObject envelope.

ui.story:
Wearable and monitor data arrives as continuous numeric streams. Storing every point as an isolated record makes it hard to retrieve meaningful context.

This strategy uses a simple pattern:
- Build one ContextObject per patient + device + time window
- Store measurements as nodes inside that ContextObject
- Keep each node payload as a standard FHIR Observation

Applications then read one document to obtain a coherent window of vitals, ready for dashboards, alerts, summarization, and enrichment.
ui.how_it_works:
Input: CSV numeric streams with time and metrics (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2).

Steps:
1) Normalize timestamps and bucket rows into fixed windows (e.g., 60s, 300s, 900s)
2) For each window, emit one ContextObject with subjects and an interval (start/end)
3) Emit nodes[] for measurements, each node containing:
   - p: tokenized path (e.g., FHIR.Observation|<code>|SpO2)
   - t: effective time (ISO timestamp)
   - data: full FHIR Observation JSON

Output: ContextObjects stored in MongoDB as the unit of record.
ui.ideal_for: Best when you need operational access to vitals streams for follow-up programs (e.g., cancer monitoring), alerting, trending, cohort filters, and AI summarization, while preserving FHIR semantics.
ui.consider_alternatives: If you only need batch analytics, a lakehouse/time-series-only approach may be enough. If you need point-by-point alerting under very tight latency, use streaming evaluation plus this strategy as the durable operational record.

terms.ContextObject: A bounded situation record storing one or more nodes (each node may embed a native resource payload).
terms.Node: A unit inside a ContextObject with canonical path token + time + payload.
terms.Windowing: Grouping samples into fixed time buckets to form deterministic ContextObjects.
terms.TelemetrySample: A normalized (patient, device, time, metric, value) event derived from raw source data.

types.title: Vitals Sample Row
types.description: A single timepoint with optional HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2. Derived from BIDMC-like dataset.

concepts.title: ContextObject Node
concepts.meaning: A node inside a ContextObject. Holds a path token, time, and payload.

concepts.title: ContextObject Envelope
concepts.meaning: ContextObject envelope containing subjects, interval, provenance, and nodes.

stores.title: Raw telemetry (optional)
stores.purpose: Optional landing store for raw rows or normalized samples.

pipeline.title: Generate FHIR Observation nodes (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2)

invariants.title: Deterministic ContextObject _id
invariants.why: Allows idempotent writes and safe replays.

invariants.title: Nodes always time-anchored
invariants.why: Required for later query and temporal reasoning.
